coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots for fluorescent resonance energy transfer recognition of methotrexate with high sensitivity and selectivity
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Weiping
Lu, Ya-Chun
Huang, Hong
Wang, Ai-Jun
Chen, Jian-Rong
Feng, Jiu-Ju
coredata.dc:description: In this report, N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots (NSCDs) were prepared by a facile, simple, low-cost, and green thermal treatment of ammonium persulfate, glucose, and ethylenediamine. The as-prepared NSCDs displayed bright blue emission with a relatively high fluorescent quantum yield of 21.6%, good water solubility, uniform morphology, and excellent chemical stability, compared to pure CDs. The fluorescence of NSCDs can be significantly quenched by methotrexate (MTX) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between NSCDs and MTX, which was used for highly selective and sensitive detection of MTX with a wide linear range up to 50.0μM and a low detection limit of 0.33nM (S/N=3). Moreover, this method was explored for practical detection of MTX in human serum with satisfied results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon nanodots
Methotrexate
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of MTX in real samples
Selectivity and interference of foreign coexisted compounds
Sensitivity of NSCDs for MTX detection
Detection of MTX in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption and photoluminescent spectra of NSCDs. Insets show the photographs taken under daylight (left) and UV light of 365nm (right). (B) Emission spectra of NSCDs recorded at different excitation wavelength from 382 to 430nm. Inset shows the normalized PL emission spectra of NSCDs.
Typical TEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of NSCDs. Inset shows the high-resolution TEM image of a single nanoparticle.
The pH effects on the PL intensities of NSCDs (A) and NSCDs-MTX (B), respectively. And the reaction-time effects of NSCDs with MTX on PL intensities (C).

(A) Fluorescence responses of NSCDs with different chemical compounds (λ ex=382nm; [X]=50.0μM). (B) The PL intensities of the mixed solution of NSCDs and some chemicals (250.0μM) before (red) and after (green) the treatment with MTX (50.0μM). Error bars were calculated in three separated groups.
Schematic illustration of the quenching mechanism of NSCDs for detection of MTX.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of NSCDs (8.0μgmL−1) by treating with different MTX concentrations. Inset shows the photographs consistent with the spectra. (B) The relationship between the F/F 0 and MTX concentrations. Error bars were calculated in three separate groups.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots for fluorescent resonance energy transfer recognition of methotrexate with high sensitivity and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geography and Environmental Science, College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Geography and Environmental Science, College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots (NSCDs) were prepared by a facile, simple, low-cost, and green thermal treatment of ammonium persulfate, glucose, and ethylenediamine. The as-prepared NSCDs displayed bright blue emission with a relatively high fluorescent quantum yield of 21.6%, good water solubility, uniform morphology, and excellent chemical stability, compared to pure CDs. The fluorescence of NSCDs can be significantly quenched by methotrexate (MTX) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between NSCDs and MTX, which was used for highly selective and sensitive detection of MTX with a wide linear range up to 50.0μM and a low detection limit of 0.33nM (S/N=3). Moreover, this method was explored for practical detection of MTX in human serum with satisfied results.

ce:section.ce:para: Methotrexate (MTX) is an important anticancer drug, which is widely used in acute leukemia and other neoplastic diseases (Guo et al., 2011; Uchiyama et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013b). However, MTX is a cytotoxic compound, acting not only in neoplastic cells but also in other cells and tissues (Asbahr et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2012). Meanwhile, patients intake MTX that sometimes bring adverse effects, owing to the increased susceptibility, excessive administration, and therapeutic errors caused by wrong intake schedules (Meesters et al., 2011). Therefore, it is of great significance to monitor MTX levels of patients in clinics and pharmaceutics.

Several methods have been used for MTX determination in biological samples, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Uchiyama et al., 2012), liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS) (Rodin et al., 2013), electroanalysis (Wang et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013a), and flow injection analysis-spectrofluorimetry coupling of derivatization and pre-concentration (Emara et al., 2013). However, HPLC and/or MS instruments are not always available in hospital laboratories.

To overcome these limitations and develop alternative approaches, fluorescence-based methods attract increasing interest because of their intrinsically high sensitivity, simplicity, and ease of operation. Particularly, metal-based nanoparticles are often employed as fluorescence probes (Chen et al., 2012), which would introduce a new kind of toxic heavy metal ions. Therefore, metallic nanoparticles can be hardly use as fluorescent probes in generally-accepted methods.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) have received great attention for their sensing ability. This is attributed to their chemical inertness and stability, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, tunable excitation and emission spectra, and good biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2013; Muthukumar et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2013). At the same time, CDs are thought as the most promising candidates in nanomaterials, owing to their better fluorescence and electronic properties (Cao et al., 2013). Accordingly, many synthetic methods have been developed, including microwave (Jiang et al., 2012), pyrolysis (Huang et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2013), and ultrasonication (Li et al., 2011a). Among them, pyrolysis is one of the most simple and efficient methods (Yang et al., 2011).

Recently, many efforts have been focused on synthesis of nitrogen (N) and/or sulfur (S) doped CDs, owing to their good performances in fluorescence and electroanalysis (Sun et al., 2013). Specifically, heteroatoms doped CDs remain almost all the advantages of blank CDs, and efficiently avoid their self-quenching (Wu and Yan, 2013) and relatively low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (Dong et al., 2013). Zhao et al. synthesized N-CDs for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging (Zhao et al., 2014). Kwon et al. prepared S-incorporated CDs, which exhibited enhanced absorbance and PL intensity in the long-wavelength regime (Kwon et al., 2013). Guo et al. fabricated N, S-codoped graphene, which demonstrated remarkably improved electrocatalytic activity and electrochemical sensing performances (Guo et al., 2013). Our group constructed NSCDs by direct thermal decomposition of gentamycin sulfate for cellular imaging (Huang et al., 2014).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a process that electronic excited state energy transfers between two chromophores, of which one acts as donor while the other as acceptor (Li et al., 2011b). It is widely used in fluorescence analysis such as bioanalysis, medical diagnosis, and optical imaging to exploit the mechanisms of biological processes, mainly owing to its non-interference characteristics. Interestingly, FRET coupled with fluorometry is usually employed for probing molecular dynamics, kinetic and photophysical properties, as illustrated in literature (Cui et al., 2008; Sahoo, 2011; Shang et al., 2007).

In this study, a simple, low-cost, and green approach was developed for preparation of NSCDs through thermal treatment of ammonium persulfate, glucose, and ethylenediamine. The fluorescent properties of NSCDs were investigated in some detail and extended as a fluorescent probe for MTX detection in human serum.

Ammonium persulfate, glucose (Glu), and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methotrexate (MTX), dopamine (DA), adrenaline (Ad), caffeine, tetracaine (TC), glutathione (GSH), streptomycin (STM), melamine (MA), l-phenylalanine (l-Phe), folic acid (FA), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), theocin, uric acid (UA), l-cysteine (l-Cys), theobromine (Theobro), riboflavine (Ribo) and rifampicin (Rifa) were received from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. Twice-distilled water was used in the whole experiments.

For typical preparation of NSCDs, 0.5g ammonium persulfate, 0.5g glucose, and 1.0mL ethylenediamine mixed together under stirring. The mixture was put into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 200°C for 2h (Fig. S1, Supporting information) and cooled to room temperature in air. The product was dissolved with water, and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min to discard the nonfluorescent deposit. The supernatant was collected and further purified with 0.22μM filter membrane, and diluted to prepare NSCDs suspensions with a concentration of 40.0mgmL−1.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fluorescence emission spectroscopy was carried out on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, UK). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were conducted on an Edinburgh FLS 920 photocounting system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Philips PW3040/60 automatic powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. Zeta potentials were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS dynamic light scattering system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were taken on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired on a Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.6eV).

For the typical assay of MTX, 0.5μL of the NSCDs suspension (40mgmL−1) was diluted with 2.5mL of water, followed by the addition of different concentrations of MTX. The mixed solutions are remained static, reacted for 5min, and finally recorded the associated fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 382nm at room temperature.

The selectivity of NSCDs was examined as a probe by some interfering compounds under the identical conditions, such as DA, Ad, caffeine, TC, GSH, STM, MA, l-Phe, FA, SMZ, theocin, UA, l-Cys, Ribo, Rifa, and theobro. The concentrations of MTX and other interfering compounds are 50.0μM. For studying the interference, the NSCDs suspension was mixed with MTX in the absence and presence of other interferent chemicals with the concentration of five times as that of MTX. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded after incubation of 5min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MTX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were separated from the blood by centrifugation for 10min at 12,000rpm, and then stored at −20°C. An aliquot of 1mL of the serum was extracted with 2mL trichloromethane–isopropanol (95:5, V/V) and centrifuged for 10min at 4000rpm. Then, the combined extract phases were evaporated to dryness under a gentle stream of nitrogen at room temperature. The dried extract was dissolved into 100μL Tris–HCl buffer (25mM, pH 7.0) before detected.

Fig. 1 shows the optical properties of the as-prepared NSCDs. Specifically, there is an absorption peak centered at 343nm for NSCDs, and exhibits the excitation spectrum at 382nm (Fig. 1A). The aqueous suspension exhibits yellow in daylight and bright-blue color under UV excitation at 365nm, respectively (inset in Fig. 1A).

As illustrated in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence spectra of NSCDs are positively shifted by adjusting the excitation wavelength from 382 to 430nm, accompanied with the great decrease of the PL intensities. It reveals the excitation-dependent PL behavior of NSCDs, as also manifested by the corresponding normalized PL emission spectra with the red shifts of the emission peaks (inset in Fig. 1B). This phenomenon is consistent with previous reports (Sahu et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2013).

The fluorescence quantum yield is calculated to be 21.6%, using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4, λ ex=382nm) as a standard. This value is comparable to previous work (Sahu et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2013), but larger than those prepared from soya bean (Li et al., 2013) and flour (Qin et al., 2013). Nevertheless, this value is smaller than NSCDs reported previously (Dong et al., 2013), probably owing to the difference of binding configurations of S atoms.

Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of NSCDs is determined to be 8.1±0.2ns (Fig. S2, Supporting information). This value is larger than those in literature (Kwon et al., 2013; Peng et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2012). Therefore, the as-prepared NSCDs have potential applications in lifetime-based bioimaging and biosensing.

Representative TEM image shows the spherical structures of NSCDs with an average diameter of 2.81nm ( Fig. 2A and B). And the clear lattice fringes with an inter-fringe distance of 0.226nm are clearly observed from HR-TEM image (inset in Fig. 2A), which is assigned to the (100) planes of graphite carbon (Kwon et al., 2013). These observations are consistent with those prepared from epoxy-enriched polystyrene (Guo et al., 2012), but different from those obtained by ascorbic acid (Jia et al., 2012).

XRD spectrum of NSCDs shows a diffraction peak located at 21.7° (Fig. S3A, Supporting information), indicating the interlayer spacing of the (002) diffraction peak with the value of 0.41nm. This value is similar to that of carbon nanoparticles prepared from acid treatment of carbon fiber (Peng et al., 2012), while it is lower than that of graphite (0.34nm). These results are possibly ascribed to the coexisted oxygen-containing functional groups and amine groups at the edges of NSCDs that enlarges the spacing of carbon layers.

FT-IR spectra were provided to further indentify the functional groups of NSCDs (Fig. S3B). The peaks emerged at 2940 and 3400cm−1 are attributed to the characteristic absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations (Qu et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2013), showing the presence of plentiful hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of NSCDs, as also supported by the peak at 1648cm−1 originated from the C=O stretching vibrations. The good hydrophilicity of NSCDs is strongly manifested by the associated Zeta potential measurements with a value of −10.6mV. Besides, the peaks at 1520 and 1340cm−1 correspond to the N–H and COO− groups, respectively. And the peak at 2100cm−1 is attributed to the C–N stretching vibrations. Particularly, the strong peak at 1100cm−1 is come from the –SO3− bonds (Sun et al., 2013).

XPS analysis was employed to examine the surface composition and oxidation state of NSCDs. As shown in Fig. S4A, the signals of O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s, and S2p elements are predominated in survey spectrum of NSCDs, with the associated contents of 15.2%, 14.4%, 69.2%, and 1.2%, respectively. High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum is divided into three peaks at the binding energies of 284.5, 285.2, and 287.7eV (Fig. S4B), corresponding to the C=C, C–O and C=O groups, respectively. Similarly, the N1s spectrum shows two peaks at 398.7 and 399.4eV (Fig. S4C), which are assigned to the C–N–C and N–(C)3 groups, respectively. And the S2p spectrum displays three peaks at 167.6, 168.5, and 169.3eV, which are come form the –C–SOx− (x=2, 3, 4) groups, as supported by the above FT-IR data. In addition, the spectrum of O1s reveals two peaks at 530.8 and 532.2eV, which are associated with the C–O and C=O groups, respectively. For more information of the functional properties of NSCDs (curve d) relative to CDs (curve a), N-CDs (curve b), and S-CDs (curve c), the corresponding FT-IR and XPS spectra are provided (Figs. S5 and S6, Supporting information). Additionally, this simple synthesis approach has high repeatability, as shown in Table S1 (Supporting information).

To check the applications of NSCDs in biological analysis, the solvent surrounding, stability, and response time were investigated by monitoring the PL intensity. It is found that the PL intensity of NSCDs and NSCDs-MTX slightly depend on the pH values ( Fig. 3) and NaCl concentrations (Fig. S7, Supporting information). And the PL quenching reaches constant within 5min. These results indicate that NSCDs have good chemical stability. Taken the PL intensity, operation complexity, and practical sensing applications together, Tris–HCl buffer (25mM, pH 7.0) and reaction time of 5min were used in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of foreign coexisted compounds
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of NSCDs as a fluorescence probe, their PL intensities were analyzed in the presence of a series of drugs, including DA, Glu, Ad, caffeine, TC, GSH, STM, MA, l-Phe, SMZ, theocin, UA, l-cys, Ribo, Rifa, Theobro, and FA. Obviously, MTX seriously decreases the PL intensity of NSCDs, while negligible changes are noticed for other chemicals under the same conditions, as listed in the graph ( Fig. 4A). Impressively, FA has much weaker effects on the PL intensity of NSCDs, despite its chemical structure similar to MTX (Table S2, Supporting information). As known, the protonation and de-protonation of the carboxylic acid residues and NH2-groups are strongly dependent on the surrounding media. In our work, some carboxylic acid and sulfonic acid residues are de-protonated in Tris–HCl buffer (25mM, pH 7.0), since the associated Zeta potential is −10.6mV at pH 7.0. On the other hand, the NH2-groups of MTX and FA are hardly protonated, because the pKa values are 4.5~5.8 for MTX and 4.7 for FA. As a result, strong hydrogen bonds interactions between NSCDs and MTX would play the critical roles for fluorescence quenching of NSCDs via FRET recognition, as displayed in Fig. 5. Nevertheless, the weaker hydrogen bonds interactions between FA and NSCDs are responsible for negligible fluorescence quenching of NSCDs under the same conditions.

On the basis of the theory of Förster's non-radioactive resonance energy transfer, the quenching mechanism can be described by the distance of donor with receptor (Geißler et al., 2013). The parameters are calculated based on the following equations:
(1)
E =
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
r
6
where E is the FRET efficiency, R 0 is the critical distance at the E of 50%, and r is the distance between receptor and donor.
(2)
R 0 6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 25
K
2
N
− 4
Φ J
where K2 is the orientation factor of the two transition dipole moments, N is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (e.g. the solvent), Φ is the luminescence quantum yield of the donor, and J is the associated spectral overlap integral.
(3)
J =
∫ 0 ∞
F ( λ ) ε ( λ )
λ
4
d λ
∫ 0 ∞
F ( λ ) d λ
where F(λ) is the intensity of donor at the wavelength of λ, ε(λ) is the molar absorption coefficient of the acceptor at the wavelength of λ.
(4)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
where F is the intensity of donor in the presence of receptor, and the donor concentration is equal to that of the receptor.

To speculate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, some parameters about FRET were evaluated based on the Eqs. (1)–(4). Under the experimental conditions, it has been reported that K 2=2/3, N=1.336 (Cui et al., 2008), and Φ=0.216 for NSCDs. Therefore, the value of the overlap integral is calculated to be 7.66×10−11 cm3 M−1. Besides, the E, R 0, and r are estimated to be 0.0044, 1.13nm, and 2.78nm, respectively. Obviously, the R 0 and r are smaller than 7nm, because of the existence of hydrogen bonding interactions. These results agree well with the conditions of Förster's non-radioactive energy transfer theory, showing the static quenching occurred between NSCDs and MTX.

Meanwhile, the interference studying between NSCDs and MTX was also performed under the identical conditions. Increasing the concentration of other drugs even to 250.0μM, the fluorescence intensities are almost kept unchanged, while the fluorescence intensities is significantly decreased with 50.0μM MTX (Fig. 4B). These results indicate the existence of efficient FRET between NSCDs and MTX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of NSCDs for MTX detection
ce:section.ce:para: Importantly, the fluorescence spectra of NSCDs are recorded after the addition of different concentrations of MTX (up to 50.0μM) at the time interval of 5min. The PL intensity at 450nm gradually decreases with MTX. This observation is in good accordance with the fluorescence quenching photographs (inset in Fig. 6A), in which the solution color gradually changes from bright blue to dark blue under the identical conditions. These findings are ascribed to the fact that the existed MTX can quench the fluorescence of NSCDs via FRET from NSCDs to MTX. Furthermore, F/F 0 shows linear responses to MTX concentrations within 50.0μM, as described by the Stern–Volmer equation: F 0/F=1+K sv[Q], where F 0 and F are the PL intensities of NSCDs in the absence and presence of MTX, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of MTX, and K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant. The detection limit (3σ) for MTX is calculated to be 0.33nM (Fig. 6B). The performance of the as-developed method is comparable with others reported previously (Table S3, Supporting information). It is clearly demonstrated the present method with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MTX in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was applied for practical detection of MTX in real samples such as human serum. The mixed solution of trichloromethane and isopropanol was added to the human serum samples for the removal of proteins in the samples, followed by the solvent evaporation. As a result, the samples were further enriched. It is found that the PL intensities of NSCDs almost remain constant by increasing the amounts of the treated serum sample (data not shown). It demonstrates that no MTX is found in the human serum samples. However, the PL intensities greatly decay by increasing the amounts of the spiked human serum sample in the presence of different concentrations of MTX. As shown in Table S4 (Supporting information), the recoveries of the three samples prepared independently are satisfied, demonstrating the applicability of the as-developed method.

In summary, NSCDs were prepared by a facile, simple, low-cost, and green thermal treatment of ammonium persulfate, glucose, and ethylenediamine. The as-prepared NSCDs displayed bright blue emission, and have good water-solubility, excellent chemical stability, and have a relatively high quantum yield of 21.6%. These superior properties endow NSCDs as a fluorescent probe for MTX detection with high sensitivity and selectivity, along with the low detection limit (0.33nM, S/N=3). The strategy relies on FRET recognition by hydrogen-bonding interactions. More importantly, the present synthetic strategy was explored for MTX detection in human serum with satisfied results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21275130, 21175118, 21475118, 21275131, 21305128 and 21345006), and Zhejiang Province University Young Academic Leaders of Academic Climbing Project (No. pd2013055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.09.066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mater. Sci. Eng. C: Mater. Biol. Appl.
